message:
feat(fp-expr): chained comparison parses to pandas-style pairwise AND

Teach the fp-expr Pratt-style query parser to handle chained
comparisons (`a < b <= c`, `1 < x < 10`, etc.) by lowering them
into the equivalent pairwise-AND tree, matching pandas / Python
"a < b < c" semantics where the expression is true iff every
adjacent comparison is true.

Implementation
--------------
Previously parse_comparison() consumed at most one comparison
operator: it called parse_add(), then peeked one comparison
token, and either constructed a single Expr::Compare or returned
the bare left operand. A second comparison token after the right
operand caused the parser to stop processing and return early,
which silently truncated chained input or surfaced as a
follow-on parse error.

Now parse_comparison() loops over adjacent comparison operators,
folding them into a left-deep AND tree:

    a < b <= c
    => And { Compare<a, b, Lt>, Compare<b, c, Le> }

    1 < x < 10
    => And { Compare<1, x, Lt>, Compare<x, 10, Lt> }

After each comparison we shift left ← right so the next
iteration's left-hand side is the previous right-hand side
(matching how Python evaluates `a OP1 b OP2 c` as
`a OP1 b and b OP2 c`). The shared `b` is currently re-evaluated
through .clone() rather than CSE-shared; that's a deliberate
keep-it-simple choice — fp-expr already clones operand trees in
its lowering pipeline and the chained input is rare in query
strings. We can intern shared sub-expressions later if a profile
flags it.

The unfolded AND tree means downstream lowering, evidence ledger,
and runtime evaluators don't need any awareness of chained
comparison — they keep seeing pairs of Compare nodes joined by
Expr::And, exactly the existing shape.

Tests
-----
- parse_chained_comparison_lowers_to_pairwise_and: parse-tree
  shape check on `a < b <= c`, asserting And{Compare<a,b,Lt>,
  Compare<b,c,Le>} with the correct SeriesRef bindings on each
  side.
- query_str_chained_comparison_matches_pandas_pairwise_semantics:
  end-to-end query_str() test with three Int64 series; runs
  `a < b < c` against a 3-row frame where exactly rows 0 and 2
  satisfy the chain (1<2<3, 3>1, 3<5), and asserts result.len()==2
  and the surviving 'a' values are [1, 3] — proving runtime
  parity with pandas chained-comparison semantics.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
